APP_ID: Pukekohe High School - Design Problem and Brief
APP_TITLE: Writing a Design Problem, Brief and Specification
APP_SUBTITLE: Learning Assessment - Clear Language, Clear Evidence

ASSESSMENTS.title: Stage 1 – Writing a Design Problem and Brief
ASSESSMENTS.subtitle: Task 1

questions.id: Write a Design Problem and Brief
questions.text:
Describe the design problem and write a brief for your project.

In your response, you must:
- clearly explain what the problem or need is
- identify who the stakeholder(s) are
- explain why this is a problem and why it needs to be solved
- describe the context or situation where the problem exists
- explain what the design outcome needs to achieve (do not describe your final solution)
- include any constraints or limitations that will affect the design

Your answer should focus on the need, not the solution, and should guide the rest of your project.
questions.hint:
To PASS, your response must clearly show that you understand the PROBLEM and the BRIEF.

Check your work against these questions:

DESIGN PROBLEM
• Have you clearly stated what the problem or need is?
• Have you said who is affected by the problem?
• Have you explained WHY this is a problem and why it needs to be solved?
• Have you described where or when the problem occurs?

DESIGN BRIEF
• Have you explained what the outcome needs to achieve?
• Have you used phrases like: “The outcome needs to…”, “It must…”, or “It is required to…”?
• Have you avoided describing what your final solution looks like?

CONSTRAINTS
• Have you mentioned at least one limitation (time, cost, materials, tools, size, safety, or school rules)?

Before submitting, check that:
✓ You mention the problem, the users, and the context
✓ You explain why the problem matters
✓ You focus on NEEDS, not your solution idea

If any of these are missing, you are unlikely to pass.

questions.text:
Write a specification for your design outcome.

Your specification must:
- be written as a list of clear criteria
- use measurable or testable language
- describe what the outcome must achieve
- include constraints and requirements

Do NOT describe your final solution.
questions.hint:
To PASS, your specification must be written as a clear list of criteria (bullet points or numbered).

Each point should:
• Start with: “The outcome must…” or “It must…”
• Be measurable or testable (include numbers where possible)
• Focus on what it must DO or achieve — NOT what it will look like

A strong specification usually includes criteria for:
1) Function – What must it do? (e.g., The outcome must securely hold 4 textbooks.)
2) User – Who must it work for? (e.g., The outcome must be suitable for Year 9–10 students.)
3) Size – Dimensions or limits (e.g., Must be no larger than 400mm x 300mm.)
4) Materials – What is allowed or restricted (e.g., Must use sustainable timber available in the workshop.)
5) Safety – What risks must be reduced or avoided (e.g., Must have no sharp edges.)
6) Durability – How strong or long-lasting (e.g., Must support at least 10kg without bending.)
7) Cost/Time – Budget or deadline limits (e.g., Must cost under $30 and be completed within 3 weeks.)
8) Sustainability – Waste, reuse, environmental impact.
9) Testing – How you will check success (e.g., It will be tested by loading it with 10kg for 1 minute.)

Avoid vague statements like:
✗ It should look cool.
✗ It should be good quality.
✗ I will make it out of plywood.

Instead, make every point specific and measurable.

ASSESSMENTS.id: PHS Hard Materials Keyword MC Quiz Part 1
ASSESSMENTS.title: Stage 3A – Hard Materials Key Word Multiple Choice Quiz
ASSESSMENTS.subtitle: Materials and Processing Technology – Key Vocabulary | Questions 1–30

questions.text: What does authentic context mean in Technology?
questions.hint: Think about whether the need is connected to a real person, place or situation.

rubric.check: ^A real-life situation where a product is needed$

options: A made-up activity with no real user
A drawing copied from the internet
A real-life situation where a product is needed
A list of tools in the workshop

questions.hint: Think about who the final product is actually for.

rubric.check: ^The person or group who will use the final outcome$

options: The machine used to make the product
The person who sells the materials
The teacher who marks the work
The person or group who will use the final outcome

questions.hint: Stakeholders can help shape the outcome during development.

rubric.check: ^Someone who has an interest in the outcome or can give useful feedback$

options: Someone who has an interest in the outcome or can give useful feedback
A tool used to hold timber
A safety rule in the workshop

questions.hint: The brief guides the project.

rubric.check: ^A short statement explaining what needs to be made and why$

options: A final photo of the product
A short statement explaining what needs to be made and why

questions.hint: Specifications help you test success.

rubric.check: ^A clear and measurable requirement the outcome must meet$

options: A clear and measurable requirement the outcome must meet
A rough sketch with no labels

questions.text: Which of these is the best example of a specification?
questions.hint: Look for the option that is specific and testable.

rubric.check: ^It must hold at least four whiteboard markers$

options: It must hold at least four whiteboard markers

questions.text: In Technology, what does outcome mean?
questions.hint: The outcome is what is developed to meet the brief.

rubric.check: ^The final product, model, design or solution being developed$

options: The final product, model, design or solution being developed

questions.text: What does fit for purpose mean?
questions.hint: Think about whether the outcome successfully does the job it was designed for.

rubric.check: ^The product works for the user, purpose and environment$

options: The product uses the most expensive material
The product works for the user, purpose and environment
The product is the most colourful

questions.hint: Think about where the product needs to work.

rubric.check: ^The place or situation where the outcome will be used$

options: The place or situation where the outcome will be used
The place where the teacher marks the work

questions.hint: Materials include things like timber, acrylic and mild steel.
options: The method used to shape a product
What an outcome is made from

questions.text: Which of these is a material?
questions.hint: Choose the option that is something an outcome could be made from.
Properties help you choose suitable materials.

rubric.check: ^A quality or characteristic of a material$

options: A quality or characteristic of a material

questions.text: Which of these is a material property?
questions.hint: A property describes how a material behaves or performs.
Techniques are practical making processes.

rubric.check: ^A method or process used to make, shape or join something$

options: The final mark for an assessment
A method or process used to make, shape or join something

questions.text: Which of these is a technique?
questions.hint: Choose the making process.
Materials are what the outcome is made from.
Techniques are processes used to make or modify the outcome.
Functional attributes describe what the outcome needs to do or how it performs.
Think about function and performance.

rubric.check: ^Something the outcome must be able to do or achieve$

options: Something the outcome must be able to do or achieve

questions.text: Which is a functional attribute for a phone stand?
questions.hint: A functional attribute links to what the product needs to do.
options: It holds a phone upright safely
It is stored in a cupboard

questions.hint: Refining means improving the outcome during development.

rubric.check: ^To improve or change something after feedback or testing$

options: To improve or change something after feedback or testing
To copy the first idea exactly

questions.hint: Justifying is stronger than just naming what you did.

rubric.check: ^Give clear reasons for a decision using evidence$

options: Give clear reasons for a decision using evidence

questions.hint: Evaluation should include a clear judgement.
options: Copy a sentence from the internet

questions.hint: Analysis explains relationships and reasons.

rubric.check: ^Break something down and explain the connections$

options: Break something down and explain the connections

questions.text: What does kaitiakitanga mean in a Technology project?
questions.hint: Think about guardianship and responsible decisions.

rubric.check: ^Care for people, place, resources and the environment$

options: Use the newest tool every time
Care for people, place, resources and the environment

questions.text: Reusing offcuts is an example of what?
questions.hint: Think about reducing waste.

questions.text: Choosing the smallest suitable sheet size to reduce waste is mainly about what?
questions.hint: This links to efficient material use.
Feasible means achievable in the real situation.

rubric.check: ^The outcome can realistically be made with the available time, tools, skills and materials$

options: The outcome can realistically be made with the available time, tools, skills and materials
The outcome is impossible to make

questions.text: Which is the best example of testing?
questions.hint: Testing checks whether the outcome performs as required.

rubric.check: ^Measuring how much weight a shelf can hold$

options: Measuring how much weight a shelf can hold
Writing your name on the page
Choosing a colour because it looks nice

questions.text: Which is the best example of trialling a technique?
questions.hint: Trialling helps compare possible making methods.

rubric.check: ^Testing screws and rivets to see which joint is stronger$

options: Testing screws and rivets to see which joint is stronger
Picking the first idea and never changing it
